commit.message:
fix: harden VS Code session support (follow-up to #35)

Addresses quality issues found in triple-model review (Opus + GPT-5.4):

Security:
- Prototype pollution guard in JSONL patch unwrap (DANGEROUS_KEY_SEGMENTS)
- sessionId extraction bounded to pre-"requests" prefix to prevent nested spoofing
- Path restriction via isAllowedSessionPath with chatSessions depth check

DRY:
- Extract filterSessionFiles() for .json/.jsonl dedup (was copy-pasted 3x)
- Extract findVSCodeSessionFiles() shared between routes and MCP server
- Remove duplicated discovery logic from mcp/server.js

Correctness:
- getFilesTouched() now includes toolInput.file for VS Code file_edit events
- buildMetrics() returns null cost for unknown/non-Claude models (no more $0.00)
- getSessionCost() returns null for Copilot/VS Code with missing cost data
- DeltaBadge handles null values without NaN or division by zero
- InboxView "Discovered" count shows total filtered, not truncated page count
- readVSCodeSessionPreview extracts sessionId from head + title from head+tail

Tests:
- New sessionsRoute.test.js: filterSessionFiles, path restrictions, sessionId
  spoofing, field extraction edge cases (24 tests)
- Prototype pollution guard test in vscodeSessionParser
- Cost model tests: null model, non-Claude model, unknown Claude variant, PRU

Docs:
- README: VS Code paths, auto-detect table formatting, inbox description
- Cost comments clarify per-format model (Claude estimate vs Copilot/VS Code null)

commit.message:
Pipeline health, backoff fixes, and documentation

- Enhanced Hephaestus API timeout handling with exponential backoff + jitter
  * call_api(): Smart error classification, fixed exponent formula, added jitter to prevent thundering herd
  * forge_one_with_retry(): Item-level retry wrapper giving API time to recover before discarding items
  * Updated _forge_batch() to use retry wrapper (3 retries per item on API failures)
  * Result: Slower processing preferred over item loss during API degradation

- Fixed Nemesis adversarial results writer file locking issue
  * reporter.py: Added error handling and file removal before write
  * Prevents crashes when file handles aren't fully released
  * Gracefully continues pipeline if write fails

- Created comprehensive Charon project README
  * Documented 5-stage architecture (Crossing → Embedding → Testing → Classification → Hypothesis)
  * Phase 1 results: 100% bridge recovery but revealed discrete representation limitation
  * Three directions forward: enriched coefficients, L-function zeros, or relationship graph embedding

- Documented health monitoring scripts for Pipeline Orchestrator
  * check_intelligence_pipeline.py: 7-agent intelligence chain monitoring + API health
  * check_forge_pipeline.py: 3-agent forge chain + backlog tracking + API timeouts
  * health_dashboard.py: Combined dashboard with optional archival
  * HEALTH_MONITORING_SCRIPTS.md: Complete reference guide

- Updated Pipeline Orchestrator role documentation
  * Added API timeout handling section to RESPONSIBILITIES.md
  * Documented break-glass authority and automatic backoff behavior

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
Add Phase 1: component build step to Cast workflow

Implements step 1 of the image generation cycle: ensure all components
are built before attempting to generate images.

For each unique component across all requested layers:
  kiln --target=<component> purge fetch checkout configure build install test package --push

If not --keep-staging, append 'purge' to clean up intermediate files.

Features:
- Parses bootstrap.toml to get component lists per image layer
- Collects all unique components across layers
- Respects --dry-run flag (shows what would be built, doesn't build)
- Respects --keep-staging flag (skips final purge if set)
- Uses crucible/config.py for Vault URL via forge.toml
- Validates bootstrap.toml structure and presence of requested layers
- Clear verbose logging of build progress

Phase structure updated:
  0. Load bootstrap.toml (parse component lists)
  1. Ensure components are built (kiln invocation loop)
  2. Resolve components (kiln resolve)
  3. Fetch and filter (cache read + role filtering)
  4. Assemble images (squashfs generation)
  5. Push to Vault (optional)

Workflow example:
  cast --dry-run --verbose
    → Shows 32 unique components to build, lists them all
  cast base
    → Builds all base components, then generates base.sqsh
  cast --keep-staging
    → Builds components, keeps staging dirs for inspection

commit.message:
Add Cast tool design documentation

Define Cast's role as image generation tool that consumes Kiln artifacts
and produces deployable images (squashfs MVP, OCI future).

CLI Interface:
  cast [OPTIONS] [LAYER ...]
  Layers: base, tools (default: both)
  --spec bootstrap.toml (auto-discovery)
  --output-dir ./images
  --vault URL
  --push (to Vault)

Debug capabilities:
  --verbose (show each step)
  --dry-run (validate without building)
  --keep-staging (inspect extracted files)
  --debug (include debug symbols)

MVP workflow:
  1. Read bootstrap.toml (component lists + role filters)
  2. kiln resolve <components> (get artifact manifest)
  3. Fetch artifacts from cache (.tar.zst + .files.json.zst)
  4. Filter by role (runtime, dev, tool, config, doc, debug, exclude)
  5. Assemble squashfs images
  6. Optionally push to Vault

Design principles:
- Declarative configuration (bootstrap.toml is source of truth)
- Non-invasive (separate tool, no Kiln changes)
- Debuggable (clear logging, dry-run, inspection modes)
- Efficient (topo order, streaming, single-pass filtering)
- Extensible (OCI containers, custom image targets in future)

Not a developer tool, but must be debuggable for both tool troubleshooting
and workflow understanding.

Define base vs tools image split for Forge bootstrap

Establishes architectural principle: components are classified by architecture
specificity rather than function.

BASE IMAGE (27 components):
- Core POSIX utilities (bash, coreutils, findutils, grep, sed, gawk)
- Arch-independent build tools (make, ninja, cmake, flex, bison, m4)
- Compression utilities (gzip, bzip2, xz, tar, zlib)
- Runtime abstractions (glibc, ncurses, readline, openssl)
- Scripting interpreters (perl, python3)
- System basics (filesystem, linux-headers, patch)
Principle: Portable across architectures (x86_64, aarch64, etc.)

TOOLS IMAGE (6 components):
- gcc, binutils (ARCHITECTURE-SPECIFIC compiler toolchain)
- GCC dependencies (gmp, mpfr, mpc, libxcrypt)
- Duplicates: glibc, linux-headers (needed for compiler target specifics)
Principle: Tied to build architecture; must be rebuilt per target arch.

Role filtering:
- BASE: includes runtime, dev, tool, config, doc (excludes debug)
- TOOLS: includes runtime, dev, tool, debug (excludes config, doc)

This configuration enables Cast to generate reproducible Forge bootstrap
images by filtering components based on this split and role annotations.

commit.message:
Implement FileSpec packaging model for glibc and linux-headers

Deploy FileSpec-based role classification for two critical foundation components.

GLIBC (components/glibc/build.py):
  Add three FileSpec overrides to handle glibc's special packaging needs:

  1. gconv/ modules → runtime
     - iconv/locale conversion modules loaded at runtime via iconv_open()
     - Absence causes locale failures in downstream components
     - Path inference would skip these (in lib64/x86_64-linux-gnu/gconv/*.so)

  2. usr/libexec/** → runtime
     - Helper executables like pt_chown called at runtime
     - Path inference misses these as non-standard paths

  3. var/db/Makefile → exclude
     - Build-time locale database helper, not needed in final package

  Removed commented glob-based runtime_globs/buildtime_globs (superseded by FileSpec).
  Maintained all comments explaining glibc's multiarch layout for future reference.

LINUX-HEADERS (components/linux-headers/build.py):
  Add defensive FileSpec to explicitly mark all outputs as dev.

  FileSpec('usr/include/**', role='dev') is redundant (path inference
  handles .h files correctly) but serves as documentation that all
  linux-headers outputs are dev-only files, never runtime.

  This defensive specification prevents future errors if non-header files
  are inadvertently added to the installation.

Both components now demonstrate FileSpec usage:

commit.message:
FileSpec packaging model implementation: framework complete, ready for deployment

FileSpec infrastructure audit results:

FULLY IMPLEMENTED:
  ✅ kiln/spec.py: Seven-role FileSpec dataclass (runtime, tool, dev, doc,
     config, debug, exclude)
  ✅ kiln/verbs/packaging.py: Complete integration
     - path_role() FHS heuristics (82 lines of inference rules)
     - _resolve_role() applies FileSpec glob overrides
     - verb_package() classifies all files and outputs files.json.zst
     - Supports file exclusion (role='exclude')

READY FOR DEPLOYMENT:
  ⏳ Framework complete and operational
  ⏳ Path inference covers ~90% of common cases
  ⏳ No components currently use FileSpec (all use inference)
  ⏳ Next: identify edge-case components needing explicit overrides

EDGE CASES TO ADDRESS:
  • glibc: ld-linux dual runtime/dev classification
  • Components with overlapping .so files (versioned + unversioned)
  • Build tools that should be marked 'tool' not 'runtime'

Updated TODO to clarify: Packaging Model is framework-complete, awaiting
identification of components that need FileSpec overrides. Path inference
alone handles most components successfully.

commit.message:
Add System Design #1: How Gmail checks email uniqueness (Bloom filters)

Introduces the 15-part System Design Interview series. Covers the
full progression from naive SQL through indexed lookups, Redis caching,
Bloom filters, scalable sharding, Counting Bloom Filters, and the
production two-phase check.

Includes:
- Interactive Bloom Filter visualizer (32-bit array, k hash functions,
  add/query/reset, sliders for m and k, false positive detection)
- Shard diagram with illustrative bit arrays
- Counting Bloom Filter example table
- Complexity comparison table across all six levels
- False positive rate calculator (p = (1 - e^(-kn/m))^k)
- Interview tips grid with six actionable points
- Three marginalia notes throughout
